skill.name: vercel-react-best-practices
skill.description: A documentation skill that guides React and Next.js performance reviews using Vercel-style rules for async data fetching, bundle size, rendering, caching, and re-render reduction.
skill.summary: Apply React and Next.js performance best practices during coding, review, and refactoring.
skill.author: ZhanlinCui

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many script, command, network, storage, and heuristic hits, but review found they are Markdown documentation examples and reference links, not executable skill behavior. No prompt injection, credential exfiltration, installer script, or malicious command execution intent was found.

low_findings.title: Static script findings are documentation examples
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. Dynamic import and with-like detections appear in Markdown examples about Next.js code splitting and immutable array methods. They are not run by the skill and do not load attacker-controlled modules.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are inside fenced Markdown examples or prose. The repository exposes guidance files only, and no execution path invokes these snippets.

low_findings.title: Static external command findings are inline command references
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. Backtick detections are Markdown inline code and fenced examples such as pnpm commands, file names, imports, and API names. No shell execution implementation was found in the reviewed skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is consistently documentation syntax rather than Ruby or shell backtick execution. The listed files contain Markdown guidance, not runtime code.

low_findings.title: Static network findings are local examples and references
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. Fetch calls and hardcoded URLs are examples or documentation references. The fetch example targets a relative API path, and metadata references official React, Next.js, SWR, GitHub, and Vercel resources.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network indicators are not used by skill code to transmit data. They appear in educational examples and source reference metadata.

low_findings.title: Static sensitive-data findings are educational storage examples
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. Browser storage and key detections appear in rules that teach minimizing localStorage data, caching storage reads, and handling keyboard keys. They do not access user secrets from the evaluator environment.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The storage APIs are presented as client-side performance examples with explicit data minimization guidance. No executable agent code reads browser storage.

low_findings.title: Critical heuristic combination is not confirmed
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The dangerous combination of code execution, network, and credential access is an artifact of aggregating unrelated Markdown examples across the guide. No single executable flow combines dynamic code loading, network exfiltration, and credential access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The static heuristic is plausible for real code, but the semantic context is a compiled best-practices document. No malicious intent or connected data flow was found.

content.value_statement: React and Next.js apps often suffer from waterfalls, large bundles, and avoidable re-renders. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code concrete review rules for faster frontend work.
actual_capabilities: Identifies async data-fetching waterfalls and suggests parallelization patterns.
Recommends bundle-size improvements such as direct imports and dynamic components.
Guides Server Component composition, request caching, and serialization reduction.
Reviews client data fetching, storage usage, and global event listener patterns.
Suggests re-render and rendering optimizations for React components.
Provides rule-specific examples for bad and improved implementations.
limitations: It is a guidance skill, not an automated profiler or benchmark tool.
It does not verify runtime performance without project measurements.
Some recommendations depend on the React and Next.js versions in use.
Examples may need adaptation for custom frameworks or older browser targets.

use_cases.title: Review a Next.js Route
use_cases.description: Find server-side waterfalls, blocking side effects, and oversized client props before merging a route change.

use_cases.title: Refactor a React Component Tree
use_cases.description: Reduce unnecessary renders, narrow effect dependencies, and move state reads closer to usage.

use_cases.title: Plan Performance Work
use_cases.description: Turn broad performance goals into prioritized tasks across data fetching, bundles, rendering, and caching.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a component or page
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this React or Next.js code using the Vercel React best practices. List the highest-impact issues first and explain each fix briefly.

prompt_templates.title: Waterfall Audit
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this route, server action, or component tree for async waterfalls. Suggest where to start promises earlier or use Promise.all.

prompt_templates.title: Bundle Reduction Pass
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these imports and client components for bundle-size problems. Recommend direct imports, dynamic imports, and deferred third-party loading.

prompt_templates.title: Full Performance Refactor Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a prioritized React and Next.js performance refactor plan. Cover waterfalls, Server Components, caching, serialization, rendering, and re-renders.

output_examples.input: A page awaits user data, config, and posts sequentially before rendering the layout.
output: Start independent requests before the first await.
Use a Suspense boundary so layout renders before post content resolves.
Keep only the data-dependent section behind the loading state.

output_examples.input: A client component imports a full icon library and a heavy editor on first load.
output: Replace broad package imports with direct imports or configure optimized imports.
Load the editor dynamically when the editing surface is opened.
Preload the editor on hover or focus when user intent is clear.

output_examples.input: A dashboard has frequent resize updates and repeated localStorage reads.
output: Subscribe to derived responsive state instead of raw width changes.
Cache repeated storage reads in memory and invalidate on external changes.
Use lazy state initialization for expensive initial values.

best_practices: Prioritize waterfall removal and bundle reduction before smaller JavaScript micro-optimizations.
Check whether data and components are independent before choosing sequential awaits.
Validate recommendations against the project runtime, framework version, and user experience goals.
anti_patterns: Do not apply every micro-optimization without evidence of a real bottleneck.
Do not move large server objects across client boundaries when only a few fields are needed.
Do not lazy-load critical above-the-fold content when it would harm SEO or layout stability.

faq.question: Is this skill only for Next.js projects?
faq.answer: No. Many React rendering and JavaScript rules apply outside Next.js, but several server rules are Next.js-specific.

faq.question: Can it automatically measure performance gains?
faq.answer: No. It provides review guidance. Use profiling, bundle analysis, and production metrics to confirm gains.

faq.question: Does it require Vercel hosting?
faq.answer: No. The guidance is Vercel-style, but most recommendations apply to React and Next.js projects on any host.

faq.question: Will it change my code automatically?
faq.answer: Only if the agent using the skill is asked to edit code. The skill itself is documentation and examples.

faq.question: Which issues should teams fix first?
faq.answer: Start with async waterfalls, bundle size, Server Component boundaries, and repeated data fetching. These usually have the largest impact.

faq.question: Are all examples safe to copy directly?
faq.answer: No. Treat examples as patterns. Adapt them to your framework version, data model, and accessibility requirements.
